g.data-narration: Welcome to Circle Generating Algorithms! Let's explore how computers draw perfect circles efficiently.
Circle drawing is essential in graphics - from UI buttons to game graphics. But it presents unique challenges compared to lines.
g.text: • Discrete pixel grid vs. continuous math
• Cannot plot every point on the circle
• Must select optimal integer pixel positions

g.data-narration: A circle is all points at distance r from center. The equation relates x and y coordinates with radius.
g.text: where (xc, yc) is center, r is radius

g.data-narration: The key insight: circles have eight-way symmetry! One computed point gives us seven more automatically.
g.text: If (x, y) is on the circle, then also:

g.data-narration: The Midpoint Circle Algorithm chooses between two pixels by checking which is closer to the true circle boundary.
g.text: Start at (0, r) - the top of the circle. At each step, choose between:

g.data-narration: The decision parameter P evaluates the circle equation at the midpoint. Its sign tells us which pixel is closer.
g.text: Circle equation at midpoint M(x+1, y-0.5):

g.data-narration: The initial decision parameter P₀ = 1 - r is derived by evaluating the circle equation at the first midpoint.
After each pixel choice, P updates incrementally. The formulas avoid expensive calculations by using the previous P value.
g.text: Note: x is incremented BEFORE calculating new P

g.data-narration: Let's see the complete algorithm flow: initialize, plot symmetric points, update based on P, repeat until x reaches y.
Let's trace through drawing a circle with radius 10. Watch how P determines each pixel choice step by step.
Here's the circle being drawn visually. Points are plotted symmetrically in all eight directions simultaneously.
Bresenham's Circle Algorithm is nearly identical to Midpoint, with a slightly different initial parameter and formula variations.
Why are these algorithms better? They avoid expensive sqrt and trig functions, making circle drawing fast and efficient.
Let's summarize the key formulas and concepts we've learned about the Midpoint Circle Algorithm.
Remember these exam tips: P₀ = 1-r, P<0 means East, stop when x reaches y. Practice tracing through small radii!
g.text: ✓ Remember: P₀ = 1 - r (not to be confused with line algorithm)
✓ Always increment x first, then calculate new P
✓ Plot final point when x = y
